maintained by the cabin crew only.

q: When the weather radar system is being maintained, ……………………………………
opts: the radar must not be operated in the hangar due to the radiation hazard.
the system can be operated at reduced power.
only a qualified avionics engineer can test it.
no special precautions are required.

q: If a hydraulic pipe shows signs of chafing damage, ……………………………………
opts: it must be inspected and repaired or replaced before the system is pressurized.
chafing is acceptable if less than 10% of the pipe surface.
a temporary tape repair is acceptable.
the pipe can be used until it leaks.

q: Before performing a fuel system test, ……………………………………
opts: all electrical bonding must be verified and all ignition sources removed.
only the fuel quantity must be checked.
the test can be performed with all normal electrical systems powered.
no special precautions are required.

q: When performing an engine hot section inspection, ……………………………………
opts: the engine must be cooled sufficiently and the correct borescope procedure followed.
the engine can be inspected immediately after shutdown.
no cooling period is required for the inspection.
only the exterior of the hot section needs inspection.

q: The aircraft nose wheel tyre must be replaced when ……………………………………
opts: the tread depth reaches the wear indicator limit specified in the maintenance manual.
it shows any sign of discoloration.
it has been used for 500 flight hours.
the tyre age exceeds 2 years.

q: When the aircraft is exposed to a volcanic ash cloud, ……………………………………
opts: a thorough inspection of all engines and airframe must be performed before returning to service.
only the engines need to be inspected.
a visual wash of the aircraft is sufficient.
the aircraft can continue normally if no performance changes are noted.

q: If the aircraft's static discharge wicks are found damaged, ……………………………………
opts: they must be replaced to ensure correct discharge of static electricity.
they can be trimmed and reused.
they are non-essential and can remain damaged.
a placard is sufficient.

q: The oxygen system charging equipment must be ……………………………………
opts: approved for oxygen use and free from oil or grease contamination.
any standard gas charging equipment.
nitrogen rated.
verified to be the same brand as the oxygen system.

q: When performing maintenance on the aircraft's fuel vent system, ……………………………………
opts: all vents must be checked to be clear of obstruction and correctly installed.
only the main vent needs to be checked.
vent inspection is optional.
vents can be blocked temporarily during maintenance.

q: If the aircraft requires a special repair not covered by the SRM, ……………………………………
opts: an engineering order must be raised and the repair approved by the design authority.
the engineer may use his best judgment.
any composite repair technique can be applied.
the repair can be made using standard aircraft repair methods.

q: Before releasing the aircraft to service after a major component replacement, ……………………………………
opts: the installation must be inspected by an independent certifying engineer.
only the installing engineer needs to verify the work.
a test flight is always required.
the captain must inspect the replacement.

q: The aircraft's exterior surfaces must be kept clean because ……………………………………
opts: contamination can affect aerodynamic performance and hide structural damage.
airlines require it for aesthetic reasons.
regulatory authorities require daily washing.
it is a passenger service requirement.

q: When signing a maintenance release, the certifying engineer is declaring that ……………………………………
opts: the maintenance has been carried out in accordance with the approved data and the aircraft is airworthy.
the aircraft is ready for painting.
the aircraft has passed all scheduled inspections.
the work will be checked again at the next scheduled stop.

q: If moisture is found in the avionics bay, ……………………………………
opts: the source must be identified and the equipment dried and inspected before dispatch.
the aircraft can fly if the bay is ventilated.
only affected units need to be checked.
a note in the logbook is sufficient.

q: Ground vibration testing of the aircraft structure must be performed ……………………………………
opts: when required by the maintenance program or after major structural repairs.
before every flight.
only during D-checks.
only after engine changes.

q: The aircraft fuel tank vent tube must be ……………………………………
opts: inspected for blockage and correct installation to ensure proper fuel system operation.
visually checked from outside the aircraft only.
replaced annually.
installed pointing into the airflow.

q: If the aircraft has been in a flood or water immersion event, ……………………………………
opts: a comprehensive water damage inspection of all systems must be performed before return to service.
only the landing gear area needs inspection.
the aircraft can be used after drying.
only the avionics bay needs to be inspected.

q: The last maintenance action on a component must be ……………………………………
opts: traceable through the maintenance records to ensure accountability.
recorded only if a defect was found.
stored in the engineer's personal records.
summarized in the captain's report.

q: When a structural fastener is replaced with an oversize fastener, ……………………………………
opts: the repair must be documented and approved as an authorized repair.
only the installation is to be noted.
no documentation is required for oversize fasteners.
the repair is automatically approved by use.

q: The fuel tank sump must be drained ……………………………………
opts: at each pre-flight inspection to check for and remove water contamination.
only during A-checks.
weekly during scheduled maintenance.
only when water contamination is suspected.

q: If the aircraft is involved in a runway excursion, ……………………………………
opts: a thorough inspection of the landing gear, brakes, and structure must be performed.
only the brakes need to be inspected.
the aircraft can be dispatched if no visible damage is found.
only the tires need to be replaced.

q: The aircraft maintenance log must be retained for ……………………………………
opts: the minimum period required by the applicable regulations.
ten years only.
the life of the aircraft.
five years after the aircraft is retired.

q: Non-essential consumable items that exceed their expiry date must ……………………………………
opts: be removed from the aircraft and replaced before the next flight.
be used until visually degraded.
be authorized for use by the engineer.
be reported to the manufacturer.

q: If an engine experiences an overtemperature event, ……………………………………
opts: a borescope inspection of the turbine section must be performed before return to service.
the engine may be returned to service if the cockpit indication returns to normal.
the event must be reported to the crew after the flight.
no inspection is required if the exceedance was brief.

q: Before entering a fuel tank for maintenance, ……………………………………
opts: a confined space permit and atmospheric monitoring must be in place.
only verbal permission from the supervisor is required.
the area must be ventilated for 30 minutes only.
the technician may enter alone if the tank has been drained.

q: Aircraft tyres are inflated with nitrogen rather than compressed air because ……………………………………
opts: nitrogen is dry and does not support combustion.
nitrogen provides more cushioning at high landing speeds.
compressed air is too expensive for aviation use.
nitrogen is lighter than air, reducing tyre weight.

q: When a hydraulic system component is replaced, ……………………………………
opts: the system must be leak-tested before the aircraft returns to service.
the hydraulic fluid need not be changed unless visibly contaminated.
a pressure test is optional if the component is a direct replacement.
no functional test is required for minor components.

q: A maintenance task card is used to ……………………………………
opts: define, control, and document each individual maintenance task.
record the aircraft's flight hours and cycles only.
authorize the maintenance organisation to carry out work.
provide a cost estimate for each maintenance task.

q: If carbon brake discs become contaminated with oil or grease, ……………………………………
opts: their braking performance is degraded and they must be replaced.
they can be cleaned with a solvent and returned to service.
their performance is unaffected below the wear limit.
the contamination is acceptable if detected before the next flight.

q: After a hard landing, ……………………………………
opts: a specific inspection of the landing gear, structure, and surrounding systems must be performed.
the aircraft may continue in service if no vibration was felt.
only the landing gear tyres need to be inspected.
the inspection is deferred to the next scheduled C-check.

q: The aircraft's centre of gravity must remain within limits throughout the flight because ……………………………………
opts: a CG outside limits can make the aircraft uncontrollable or structurally unsafe.
exceeding CG limits increases fuel burn.
the pressurization system depends on CG being within range.
CG outside limits triggers autopilot disconnection.

q: Electrical bonding straps that are broken or corroded must ……………………………………
opts: be replaced immediately to restore electrical continuity.
be cleaned and retested before further action.
be monitored at the next scheduled inspection.
be replaced only if the resistance exceeds 10 ohms.

q: Before performing any maintenance on a high-voltage electrical system, ……………………………………
opts: power isolation must be verified and a lockout/tagout procedure applied.
the system may remain energized if the area is properly marked.
verbal confirmation from the pilot is sufficient authorization.
a qualified electrician must be present as an observer only.

q: The fire suppression system on an aircraft engine ……………………………………
opts: is activated by the engine fire push button in the cockpit, which isolates the engine and arms the extinguisher.
discharges automatically when the fire detection loop detects heat.
can only be activated by ground personnel.
provides unlimited fire suppression capacity.

q: If metallic particles are found in a hydraulic filter during replacement, ……………………………………
opts: a system investigation must be initiated to identify the source.
the particles are noted and the system returned to service.
the hydraulic fluid is replaced and no further action is needed.
the filter replacement interval is shortened to 50 hours.

q: Avionics equipment must be protected from electrostatic discharge because ……………………………………
opts: static electricity can damage sensitive electronic components.
static electricity reduces the battery life of avionics units.
electrostatic discharge can cause shorts in the aircraft wiring.
avionics are required by law to be stored in antistatic environments.

q: The Aircraft Maintenance Manual (AMM) must be consulted because ……………………………………
opts: it provides the approved step-by-step procedures for all maintenance tasks.
it contains the aircraft's flight performance data.
it is the only source of spare parts information.
it replaces the need for maintenance personnel qualifications.

q: During aircraft fuelling operations, the aircraft must be bonded to the fuel bowser because ……………………………………
opts: this dissipates static electricity and reduces the risk of igniting fuel vapour.
it ensures the fuel flow rate is measured accurately.
it prevents contamination of the fuel during transfer.
it is a requirement of the refuelling contract.

q: A borescope inspection is used to ……………………………………
opts: visually inspect internal engine components without major disassembly.
measure fuel flow through the engine fuel nozzles.
check hydraulic pressure inside the actuators.
inspect the external surface of the engine fan blades.

q: If the nitrogen pre-charge in a hydraulic accumulator is found too low, ……………………………………
opts: the accumulator's capacity is reduced and it must be recharged to the specified pressure.
the accumulator is serviceable if the hydraulic pressure remains normal.
the aircraft may continue in service with enhanced monitoring.
the nitrogen charge is supplemented with compressed air.

q: When an aircraft undergoes a D-check (heavy maintenance visit), ……………………………………
opts: the aircraft is fully disassembled to allow comprehensive structural inspection of the entire airframe.
only the engines and landing gear are overhauled.
the check is limited to electrical and avionics systems.
the check is performed on a sample of aircraft in the fleet only.

q: The purpose of the Illustrated Parts Catalogue (IPC) is to ……………………………………
opts: identify parts by assembly breakdown and part number for ordering and installation.
provide step-by-step maintenance procedures for each system.
list the electrical schematic of each aircraft system.
provide the aircraft's structural repair limits.

q: A component that has a "hard time" life limit ……………………………………
opts: must be replaced or overhauled at a fixed interval regardless of its apparent condition.
must be replaced only when it shows signs of wear.
may be extended beyond its limit if an inspection confirms it is serviceable.
has no condition monitoring requirements.

q: Before an aircraft is released to service after maintenance, the certifying engineer must ……………………………………
opts: confirm that all maintenance work has been correctly completed and documented.
check that the aircraft has sufficient fuel for the next flight.
ensure the flight crew is aware of the maintenance performed.
verify that all passengers have boarded the aircraft.

q: The purpose of the MEL (Minimum Equipment List) is to ……………………………………
opts: allow the aircraft to dispatch with defined inoperative systems or components under controlled conditions.
list all equipment that must be operational for every flight.
define the minimum maintenance tools required for each check.
specify the minimum qualifications for maintenance personnel.

q: If a tyre shows a flat spot caused by a locked-wheel skid, ……………………………………
opts: it must be assessed against the allowable damage limits and may need to be replaced.
it can be returned to service if the flat spot is smooth.
it is serviceable if the wheel passes a visual balance check.
it need only be replaced if the aircraft has a reported shimmy on the next landing.

q: Delamination in a composite structure ……………………………………
opts: is separation of layers within the material that may not be visible from the surface and requires NDT to assess.
is a surface defect that can be repaired with surface filler.
is always visible as a raised blister on the surface.
can be corrected by applying pressure and adhesive.

q: Engine trend monitoring is used to ……………………………………
opts: detect early signs of engine deterioration by analysing performance data over time.
record the engine's total accumulated hours for logbook purposes.
calculate the engine's remaining service life.
monitor fuel efficiency during normal cruise flight.

q: Before an aircraft can be towed, ……………………………………
opts: chocks must be removed, gear pins removed, all panels secured, and a clear communication plan established.
the pilot must confirm the tow from the cockpit.
the aircraft must have all engines running.
the fuelling operation must be completed.

q: The function of the pitot heat system is to ……………………………………
opts: prevent ice from forming on the pitot probe, which would cause incorrect airspeed indication.
heat the windshield to prevent ice accumulation.
provide warm air to the static ports to prevent condensation.
regulate the temperature of the pitot system pressure lines.

q: When performing a weight and balance calculation, the "moment" is determined by ……………………………………
opts: multiplying the weight of each item by its arm (distance from the reference datum).
dividing the total aircraft weight by the distance between the CG and the datum.
adding the weight of all items to the basic empty weight.
subtracting the zero fuel weight from the maximum takeoff weight.

q: During a pressurization test of the aircraft fuselage structure, ……………………………………
opts: the structural integrity of the fuselage under pressurization loading is verified.
only the door seals and window seals are checked.
the test is performed only if a pressurization warning has occurred.
the aircraft is pressurized to twice the normal differential pressure.

q: Replacement parts installed on an aircraft must ……………………………………
opts: have a valid serviceable release certificate and traceable part history.
only be from the same manufacturer as the part they replace.
be visually inspected by the pilot before installation.
be re-certified by the maintenance organisation before use.

q: If an aircraft exceeds its maximum landing weight on landing, ……………………………………
opts: a special inspection must be performed before the next flight.
the aircraft may continue in service if no structural damage is visible.
the flight crew must complete an incident report and no further action is needed.
the landing gear only needs to be inspected.

q: The function of the aircraft bleed air precooler is to ……………………………………
opts: reduce the temperature of the bleed air before it enters the pneumatic distribution manifold.
increase the pressure of bleed air for engine starting.
cool the cabin air before it is delivered to passengers.
regulate bleed air flow to the anti-icing system.

q: A "squawk" as used by aircraft maintenance personnel refers to ……………………………………
opts: a defect or malfunction reported by the flight crew for maintenance action.
a type of radio communication between the aircraft and ATC.
a specific warning tone activated by the aircraft's GPWS.
a maintenance schedule item.

q: FOD (Foreign Object Debris/Damage) prevention in maintenance requires ……………………………………
opts: accounting for all tools and materials before and after each maintenance task.
using only approved tools from the tool store.
conducting a walkround of the aircraft after every maintenance action.
keeping all work areas clean at the end of the maintenance check.

q: When locking wire is installed on a critical fastener, ……………………………………
opts: it must be routed so that loosening the fastener causes the wire to tighten.
the wire must be routed away from any moving parts.
the wire must be installed before the fastener is torqued.
the wire must be attached to an adjacent structure for support.

q: A "Certificate of Release to Service" (CRS) ……………………………………
opts: is issued by the certifying engineer to confirm the aircraft is airworthy following maintenance.
is issued by the regulatory authority after annual inspection.
is issued to approve the use of a new component type.
is issued by the manufacturer after delivery of a new aircraft.

q: A hydraulic system bypass valve open position means ……………………………………
opts: fluid flows around a restriction, which may indicate a blocked component or open test valve.
the system has been isolated for maintenance.
the pump is operating at maximum output.
the accumulator is fully charged and the excess pressure is relieved.

q: If an aircraft is struck by lightning, ……………………………………
opts: specific inspections covering lightning entry/exit points, fuel system, avionics, and flight controls must be completed before return to service.
the aircraft may continue operations if no pilot report of damage is received.
only the antenna and avionics need to be checked.
the aircraft is inspected at the next scheduled check.

q: The purpose of the Safety Management System (SMS) in a maintenance organisation is to ……………………………………
opts: systematically identify, assess, and mitigate safety risks through a structured process.
investigate accidents after they occur.
ensure all maintenance is completed on schedule.
provide a training framework for new technicians.

q: When performing an NDT inspection, the method selected must ……………………………………
opts: be appropriate for the material type and the type of defect being sought.
be the most cost-effective method available.
always include both ultrasonic and visual inspection.
be the method with which the technician is most familiar.

q: Before releasing the aircraft after a C-check, ……………………………………
opts: all task cards must be signed off, all components reinstalled, and a comprehensive functional check performed.
a test flight must always be performed.
the regulatory authority must inspect the aircraft.
all major components must be sent for calibration.

q: Engine-driven hydraulic pumps (EDPs) are driven from ……………………………………
opts: the engine accessory gearbox and are the primary source of hydraulic pressure.
the APU bleed air system.
the aircraft electrical bus.
the engine thrust reverser system.

q: When using dye penetrant inspection, the developer is applied to ……………………………………
opts: draw penetrant out of any cracks, making them visible as coloured indications.
clean the surface before the penetrant is applied.
set the penetrant permanently in any defects found.
increase the surface porosity for better penetrant absorption.

q: If the fire extinguisher bottle pressure on an engine is below the minimum limit, ……………………………………
opts: the system is unserviceable and must be recharged or replaced before the next flight.
the aircraft may fly for one more sector with crew awareness.
a lower pressure is acceptable if the temperature is low.
the bottle is acceptable if the other bottle is fully charged.

q: During an aircraft painting operation, ……………………………………
opts: masking must protect all areas not to be painted, and overspray must be prevented from reaching critical areas.
the aircraft must be fully fuelled to prevent moisture ingress.
all access panels must be removed to ensure full coverage.
the aircraft must be in the pressurized condition during painting.

q: The Fault Isolation Manual (FIM) provides ……………………………………
opts: systematic troubleshooting guidance to identify the cause of faults in aircraft systems.
the step-by-step procedures for replacing faulty components.
a list of all faults that have been recorded on the aircraft fleet.
the approved tools for testing each aircraft system.

q: Pitting corrosion in aluminium structures is particularly concerning because ……………………………………
opts: the small pits can be deeper than they appear and can grow into cracks under fatigue loading.
pitting always requires immediate structural replacement.
pitting corrosion cannot be detected by visual inspection.
pitting corrosion affects only cosmetic surfaces.

q: When a new Aircraft Maintenance Programme (AMP) revision is issued, ……………………………………
opts: the operator must update their maintenance procedures to comply with the new revision.
the revision takes effect only after the next scheduled C-check.
the revision applies only to new aircraft entering the fleet.
the operator may continue with the previous revision until the next renewal.

q: A "dry bay" in aircraft structure is an area where ……………………………………
opts: no flammable fluids are permitted, to reduce fire risk.
the aircraft dehumidification system vents dry air.
all drains are sealed to prevent moisture entry.
fuel tank access is prohibited for maintenance.

q: If a maintenance organisation fails to maintain its required certifications and approvals, ……………………………………
opts: it may lose the authorisation to release aircraft to service.
it must immediately ground all aircraft it has signed for.
all maintenance work performed becomes automatically invalid.
the regulatory authority issues a warning but no operational impact occurs.

q: Aircraft battery electrolyte spills must be ……………………………………
opts: neutralised with the appropriate solution before cleaning, as the electrolyte is corrosive.
cleaned with dry cloths immediately without any treatment.
reported to the regulatory authority within 24 hours.
sealed with sealant to prevent further spread.

q: The purpose of the outflow valve in the pressurization system is to ……………………………………
opts: control the rate at which cabin air is vented overboard, thereby maintaining the desired differential pressure.
supply fresh outside air to the cabin.
shut off the bleed air supply to the cabin if pressure exceeds limits.
provide a safety relief function if the fuselage is overpressurised.

q: Wing anti-icing using bleed air is more effective than de-icing fluid because ……………………………………
opts: bleed air provides continuous heating, preventing ice formation rather than removing ice after it forms.
bleed air is available on all aircraft types.
de-icing fluid degrades quickly at high altitude.
bleed air uses less energy than the de-icing system.

q: The purpose of the crew oxygen demand mask is to ……………………………………
opts: deliver oxygen on demand to crew members at high altitude when cabin pressure is lost.
provide supplemental oxygen during normal flight above 10,000 ft.
supply 100% oxygen to crew throughout all flight phases.
replace the function of the aircraft pressurization system.

q: An aircraft that is operated outside of its Approved Maintenance Programme without authorisation ……………………………………
opts: may be considered unairworthy and be grounded by the regulatory authority.
can continue operations if no defects are found.
must undergo an additional inspection to return to compliance.
will have its Certificate of Airworthiness automatically suspended.

q: When an engine is motored without ignition after a failed start, the purpose is to ……………………………………
opts: purge unburned fuel from the combustion section to prevent a hot start on the next attempt.
cool the turbine section after a high-power run.
dry the engine of any residual moisture after storage.
check that the starter is functioning correctly before the next start attempt.

q: Non-destructive testing is called "non-destructive" because ……………………………………
opts: it detects defects without damaging or permanently altering the component.
it does not require the component to be removed from the aircraft.
the test equipment cannot be damaged by the test.
it is performed outside the aircraft structure.

q: The Structural Repair Manual (SRM) is used when ……………………………………
opts: damage is found on the aircraft structure and approved repair data or allowable damage limits are required.
the aircraft undergoes a routine A-check inspection.
avionics components are replaced on the aircraft.
the weight and balance of the aircraft is recalculated.

q: Tool calibration records must be maintained by the maintenance organisation because ……………………………………
opts: calibrated tools are required to ensure accurate measurements and correct torque application.
calibration is required only for new tools when first issued.
the regulatory authority requires all tools to be certificated annually.
calibration prevents tools from being used by unauthorised personnel.

q: If a hydraulic system's fluid is found to be contaminated with water, ……………………………………
opts: the fluid must be completely drained and replaced, and the system investigated to identify the source of water ingress.
the water content is acceptable if below 0.5%.
the water can be removed by circulating the fluid through the filter.
the system is returned to service if the fluid passes a colour check.

q: The purpose of the aircraft Technical Log is to ……………………………………
opts: record all flight hours, defects, maintenance actions, and certifications throughout the aircraft's operation.
record the training hours of the maintenance personnel.
provide regulatory authorities with a summary of completed maintenance checks.
document the fuel uplifted at each refuelling stop.

q: An overspeed condition in an aircraft engine is particularly dangerous because ……………………………………
opts: centrifugal forces at excessive speed can cause plastic deformation or failure of rotating components.
the engine fuel metering unit may be damaged.
the engine oil system cannot lubricate adequately at high speeds.
the compressor blades may stall at excessive speed.

q: Before signing a maintenance task as complete, the certifying engineer must ……………………………………
opts: personally verify that the work has been carried out correctly and all referenced documentation has been followed.
ensure the aircraft has been fuelled for the next flight.
confirm that the next maintenance task has been planned.
receive approval from the quality manager.

q: The purpose of aircraft ground bonding cables is to ……………………………………
opts: equalize the electrical potential between the aircraft and ground equipment to prevent static sparks.
connect the aircraft to the main electrical supply on the ground.
secure the aircraft to the ground during high-wind conditions.
provide a return path for ground power current.

q: A serviceable life-limited component must have ……………………………………
opts: complete and traceable maintenance history with current life remaining clearly documented.
only the manufacturer's part number and serial number on a tag.
a valid test certificate from the overhaul facility.
the aircraft registration number it was removed from.

q: During aircraft jacking operations, ……………………………………
opts: the aircraft must be continuously monitored and jacking pads correctly positioned to prevent structural damage.
the engines must be running to maintain hydraulic pressure.
the aircraft may be lowered immediately if a problem occurs.
only one jack is required for stability during nose gear work.

q: When performing maintenance on the aircraft fuel system, ……………………………………
opts: all ignition sources must be removed and the area must be ventilated to prevent accumulation of fuel vapour.
the aircraft must be powered down completely.
only fire extinguishers need to be on standby.
the fuel system can remain pressurised during maintenance.

q: The APU fire detection loop must be functionally tested because ……………………………………
opts: it must be confirmed to alert the crew correctly in the event of an APU fire.
it is tested to verify that the extinguisher bottle pressure is correct.
it ensures the APU bleed air valves are working correctly.
it confirms the APU starter motor is serviceable.

q: During composite repair curing, ……………………………………
opts: the correct temperature and cure time specified in the repair data must be maintained to ensure the repair achieves full strength.
the temperature can be increased to speed up the curing process.
cure time is less important than the mixing ratio of the resin.
the repair can be used immediately after the adhesive sets.

q: The Wing Fuelling Station Valve is closed after fuelling to ……………………………………
opts: prevent fuel from draining back from the tank and to isolate the fuelling connection.
allow the fuel tanks to pressurize to operating pressure.
prevent reverse flow to the fuel quantity indicating system.
isolate the fuelling vent from the fuel tank.

q: If an aircraft component is found to be unserviceable during inspection, ……………………………………
opts: it must be tagged as unserviceable and removed from the aircraft or clearly labelled if it cannot be immediately removed.
it may remain installed if it is non-critical.
it can be used for the next flight if no replacement is available.
the flight crew should be informed and the decision left to them.

q: Magnetic particle inspection can only be used on ferrous metals because ……………………………………
opts: only ferrous materials respond to a magnetic field, which is required to reveal defects using iron particles.
ferrous metals have a higher defect rate than non-ferrous metals.
the magnetic particles would contaminate non-ferrous materials.
non-ferrous materials are too hard for the test equipment to penetrate.

q: The purpose of the aircraft's Ram Air Turbine (RAT) is to ……………………………………
opts: provide emergency hydraulic or electrical power using airstream energy if all generators fail.
cool the engine turbine section during high-power operations.
supply ram air to the cabin conditioning system.
provide backup bleed air for engine starting.

q: Flight control cables must be inspected for ……………………………………
opts: wear, corrosion, broken strands, and correct tension during each scheduled inspection.
correct colour coding during each A-check.
correct routing through the aircraft each flight.
lubrication and flexibility at each C-check only.

q: If oil is found on avionics equipment during inspection, ……………………………………
opts: the source of the oil must be identified and rectified, and the equipment assessed for serviceability.
the equipment may be cleaned and returned to service.
the equipment must be replaced regardless of apparent condition.
the source need not be identified if the quantity is small.

q: "Potting compound" in avionics connectors is used to ……………………………………
opts: protect the electrical connections from moisture and vibration.
lubricate the connector contacts to prevent fretting.
identify the connector by colour coding.
insulate the connector from the aircraft structure.

q: The purpose of the anti-skid system is to ……………………………………
opts: prevent wheel lockup during braking by modulating hydraulic pressure to each brake.
automatically apply braking on touchdown regardless of pedal pressure.
distribute braking force equally between all wheels.
protect the brake discs from overheating during prolonged braking.

q: Aircraft maintenance records are retained for the required period because ……………………………………
opts: they provide evidence that the aircraft has been maintained in an airworthy condition and support traceability of all work.
the manufacturer requires copies of all maintenance records.
they are used to calculate the aircraft's residual value.
regulatory authorities must hold copies of all maintenance records.

q: A maintenance organisation must have a tool calibration register because ……………………………………
opts: it provides evidence that all precision tools are calibrated to the required standard and are within their validity period.
calibration is required only for tools used in engine overhaul.
the calibration register is submitted to the regulatory authority annually.
it tracks the cost of tool calibration for financial reporting.

q: The function of the aircraft negative pressure relief valve in the pressurization system is to ……………………………………
opts: prevent the outside air pressure from exceeding cabin pressure during rapid descent.
provide emergency pressurization if the main outflow valve fails.
protect the fuselage from positive differential pressure exceeding limits.
vent the cabin to outside pressure during descent below 10,000 ft.

q: Before disconnecting any oxygen system component, ……………………………………
opts: the oxygen supply must be shut off and the system depressurised.
only personal protective equipment is required.
the system may be depressurised by loosening the fitting slowly.
the aircraft must be grounded on external power.

q: During an aircraft skin inspection, evidence of fluid weeping through panels ……………………………………
opts: must be investigated to identify the source of the leak.
is acceptable if the quantity is small.
requires sealing with RTV immediately.
is noted and deferred to the next major check.

q: When performing post-maintenance inspection of electrical wiring, signs of overheating such as ……………………………………
opts: discolouration, melting, or charring of insulation indicate a circuit fault that must be investigated and rectified.
slight yellowing of insulation is acceptable if the circuit is not safety-critical.
discolouration indicates the wiring is operating at its rated current.
melted insulation can be replaced with electrical tape as a temporary repair.

q: The purpose of the manufacturer's Component Maintenance Manual (CMM) is to ……………………………………
opts: provide detailed instructions for overhaul, testing, and repair of individual aircraft components.
provide the aircraft maintenance schedule.
